name: Old Mine Dump
short_description: Historic mine dump in the Adirondack foothills yielding garnets, mica, and feldspars. Flat terrain makes this an accessible collecting spot for all skill levels.
description:
The Old Mine Dump near Bellmont is an abandoned mining site in Franklin County's mineral-rich Adirondack region. This locality has produced garnets and associated pegmatite minerals for generations of rockhounders. The dump material offers excellent opportunities to find well-formed garnet specimens, sheet mica, and feldspar in a relatively flat, easy-to-explore setting.

The site lies at the edge of the Adirondack Lowlands, where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and pegmatitic intrusions dominate. The mine workings accessed pegmatite veins rich in feldspar and mica, with garnet as a common accessory mineral. Tailings and dump piles remain productive for finding loose specimens and fragments.

Expect to search through weathered rock piles and loose material. Garnet specimens here range from small crystals embedded in feldspar to larger individual pieces. Mica flakes and books are abundant. The flat terrain and exposed dump areas make this site manageable for families and beginners, though careful footing around loose rock is always necessary.

Bring a geology hammer, chisel, and small bucket or bag for specimens. The surrounding area is typical Adirondack forest, so wear insect protection in warmer months. Water and services are available in nearby Bellmont.
history: This mine dump represents Adirondack pegmatite mining activity from the 19th and early 20th centuries, when feldspar, mica, and garnet were extracted for industrial and ornamental use. The Precambrian-age pegmatite host rocks crystallized during the Grenville Orogeny around 1 billion years ago, and later metamorphic events concentrated valuable silicate minerals into veins that miners exploited for feldspar and mica products.
tips: Search the loose material and tailings piles systematically, checking for red and dark garnets mixed with white feldspar and clear mica sheets. Use a rock hammer to carefully split larger specimens and expose fresh surfaces where garnet crystals are more visible. Look along the edges of old mine cuts and in eroded banks where weathering has naturally exposed mineral-rich sections. Collect mica books by carefully prying them apart with a chisel - they often break along natural cleavage planes.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1535 feet with flat terrain throughout the site. The area features typical Adirondack mixed forest with scattered bedrock outcrops and mine tailings. Walking distance from parking is minimal to short on maintained informal paths. Footing is generally stable on packed earth and rock but requires care around loose rubble piles and mine debris.
best_season: May - October. The months from May through October offer the warmest, driest conditions and minimal snow. Summer months (June - August) have full access but also higher insect activity. Fall (September - October) provides comfortable temperatures and fewer bugs. Winter snow and mud make access difficult and unpleasant.
written_directions: From the village of Bellmont, take NY Route 11 north approximately 2 miles toward the town of Enosburg Falls. Turn east on a local road toward the Adirondack foothills. Continue for approximately 1 mile on increasingly rural roads until you approach the old mining area. Park in an informal pullout or cleared area near the dump site itself. GPS coordinates (44.73932, -74.172522) will guide you to the exact location. Note: Local knowledge and recent communication with nearby residents is recommended to confirm current access and parking.
road_conditions: Unpaved local roads in final approach. Passenger vehicle suitable in dry conditions. Avoid after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullout or cleared area near the mine dump. Limited parking for 2-4 vehicles. No formal facilities.
collection_rules: This is private property. Permission from the landowner is required before collecting. Respect all posted signs and property boundaries. Collect only loose specimens and surface material - do not excavate, drill, or remove in-place rock. Pack out all trash. Leave the site in good condition for future visitors.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before visiting
Collect loose specimens only - no excavation
Respect all posted signs and property boundaries
Pack out all trash
Do not remove in-place rock or damage outcrops
nearest_services: Bellmont village has basic services. Malone, NY (approximately 15 miles south) offers full amenities including gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain with minimal elevation change makes this site accessible to most visitors. Unpaved access road may be muddy after rain. Informal trails and loose rubble around dump piles require sturdy footwear and careful footing. Not wheelchair accessible due to uneven ground and loose material. Manageable for families with children ages 6 and up.

faq.question: What garnets and minerals can I find at the Old Mine Dump near Bellmont?
faq.answer: This historic dump site is productive for red to dark garnets, sheet mica, feldspar, and quartz. Garnet specimens range from small crystals to larger pieces suitable for display. Mica books are particularly abundant and often come free with light prying. Feldspar can be found as large, white, blocky crystals throughout the tailings.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the Old Mine Dump in New York?
faq.answer: No permit is required, but this is private property. You must obtain permission from the landowner before visiting and collecting. Check locally with town records or visit the site respectfully to identify who manages the property.

faq.question: What is the best time to visit this Bellmont rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Visit between May and October for the best conditions. Summer months offer warm weather but higher insect activity. Fall (September - October) provides comfortable temperatures and fewer bugs. Winter and early spring bring mud, snow, and poor access on unpaved roads.

faq.question: How do I collect mica and garnet specimens at this Adirondack mine dump?
faq.answer: Use a geology hammer and chisel to carefully work loose specimens from tailings and pile material. Look for red garnets mixed with white feldspar. For mica books, locate natural cleavage planes and gently pry them apart with a chisel - they often separate cleanly. Check eroded banks and old mine cuts where weathering has exposed fresh mineral surfaces.

faq.question: Is the Old Mine Dump rockhounding site beginner-friendly and family-safe?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is excellent for beginners and families. The flat terrain and exposed dump material make it easy to find specimens. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely with supervision. Loose rock and old mine debris present minor hazards, so wear sturdy shoes and watch footing carefully.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at this Franklin County site?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, chisel, and sturdy work gloves. A small bucket or bag is useful for carrying specimens. Wear closed-toe hiking boots or work shoes for loose rubble. From May through August, insect repellent is essential. Bring water and a hat for sun protection. A field guide to Adirondack minerals will help identification.

meta_title: Old Mine Dump Rockhounding - Bellmont, NY
meta_description: Find garnets, mica, and feldspar at this historic Adirondack mine dump. Private property access. Beginner-friendly collecting near Bellmont, NY.
